network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through literature-grounded, convergent prebiotic routes centered on (i) surface cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry in which HCN is reductively homologated to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde and threonine aminonitrile appears as a branch-product precursor, (ii) a prebiotic sugar pathway in which formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde generate tetroses that, with NH3, H2S, and thiols, rearrange to activated threonine thioesters that hydrolyze to threonine, and (iii) a speculative but literature-mentioned Mg-porphin photochemical route to stereocontrolled threonine. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a feeder network for HCN-compatible carbon sources such as formate, acetate, and pyruvate, while recognizing threonine instability at high temperature; therefore, most target-forming steps occur in cooler surface or biochemical settings. The 10 pathways are assembled from shared hub intermediates (HCN, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde/dihydroxyacetone pool, tetrose pool, and threonine aminonitrile) to satisfy network connectivity and environmental interconnection.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 to surface-bound formyl/formate species on Fe-S phases.
reactions.reasoning: Vent Fe-S catalysis for CO2 reduction to formate is consistent with iron-sulfur world and alkaline vent models used as feeder chemistry for prebiotic organics, though not a direct threonine-forming step (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Martin, 2004). This step is included conservatively to supply reduced carbon to connected environments and addresses the literature emphasis on hydrothermal carbon fixation without placing threonine formation itself in overly hot settings.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor or vent-associated FTT chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated CO hydrogenation and C-C coupling furnish small aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite/Fe-mediated Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry is supported as a source of simple aldehydes and hydrocarbons in hydrothermal systems and extraterrestrial parent-body contexts; acetaldehyde is specifically relevant because the research report identifies formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and ammonia as parent-body precursors associated with hydroxy amino acid formation (McCollom & Seewald, 2007; Aponte et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from reduced carbon
reactions.mechanism: Fe-mineral-mediated reductive carbon fixation and C-C assembly yield alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate analogs.
reactions.reasoning: The network instructions explicitly identify pyruvate synthesis from CO2/H2 on magnetite/greigite as hydrothermal chemistry; such keto-acid formation is used here as a connected hub for later biochemical transamination logic rather than as a direct threonine precursor. This is compatible with vent protometabolism concepts and reductive TCA analog discussions (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Surface-assisted imine formation from pyruvate and NH3 followed by reduction to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids on mineral surfaces is central to hydrothermal amino-acid-generating scenarios and is explicitly listed in the prompt. Alanine is retained as a connected metabolically relevant product and as a possible catalyst/participant in sugar chemistry, consistent with the research discussion that existing amino acids can catalyze aldol chemistry at moderate pH (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Surface UV cyanosulfidic homologation of HCN to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation, phosphate-bearing shallow pool, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN under H2S/Cu photoredox conditions gives C2 sugar precursor glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN, facilitated by H2S and Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling, as the core route to glycolaldehyde in the cyanosulfidic network that also leads to threonine precursors. Mineral agents are included to satisfy the schema while preserving Cu photoredox as the key chemical driver (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface UV cyanosulfidic homologation of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation, flow-chemistry style staged addition, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: A second reductive homologation extends the carbon skeleton from C2 to C3 aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Sutherland-type cyanosulfidic chemistry generates glyceraldehyde from simpler HCN-derived feedstocks under UV/H2S/Cu photoredox conditions. The report directly links this triose chemistry to inevitable formation of the threonine aminonitrile branch product (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface triose isomerization to dihydroxyacetone
reactions.mechanism: Triose isomerization gives the ketotriose dihydroxyacetone from glyceraldehyde with stabilization by mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied research specifically notes glyceraldehyde to dihydroxyacetone conversion at 59% yield within the relevant prebiotic network. Borate minerals are included because the same report emphasizes borate stabilization of sugars in surface environments, while the role of the reaction here is to encode the literature-supported triose pool rather than claim direct threonine formation from DHA alone (Patel et al., 2015; Ricardo et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Surface branch formation of threonine aminonitrile from triose cyanosulfidic chemistry

conditions.other: UV irradiation, cyanosulfidic flow chemistry, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Within the triose-generating network, C-C bond formation combined with cyanide addition and amination gives 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile as a branch product.
reactions.reasoning: The research report identifies threonine aminonitrile, 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile, as an 'inevitable byproduct' of the same chemistry that yields glyceraldehyde and dihydroxyacetone in Sutherland's cyanosulfidic protometabolism. This step is intentionally represented as a network branch rather than an over-detailed single elementary mechanism because the literature presents it as embedded systems chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: cooler surface or sediment microenvironment to limit decomposition

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide/carboxylate formation to yield free amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Although the deep report emphasizes aminonitrile formation more than explicit hydrolysis conditions, conversion of Strecker-type aminonitriles to amino acids is the standard completion step and is required to connect the documented precursor to the target. This step is therefore slightly connective but chemically orthodox, and cooler settings are chosen because the report highlights threonine thermal fragility (Miller, 1957; Bada, 1991; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface aldol condensation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to tetrose pool

conditions.catalyst: ammonia-catalyzed aldol chemistry on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: mildly acidic to near-neutral, around pH 5.2, wet-dry concentration cycles

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia-catalyzed aldol condensation and isomerization generate a tetrose pool including erythrose, threose, and erythrulose.
reactions.reasoning: The prebiotic sugar pathway described in the research begins with formaldehyde plus glycolaldehyde to produce tetrose sugars under mild aqueous conditions, with ammonia or amino acids acting as catalysts at approximately pH 5.2. This reaction is included in that literature-grounded form rather than as a generic high-pH formose step (Weber, 1987; Weber, 2001).

reactions.name: Surface dehydration of tetrose pool to alpha-ketoaldehyde

conditions.other: concentrating evaporitic or interlayer clay microenvironment

reactions.mechanism: Tetrose sugars undergo beta-dehydration to the corresponding alpha-ketoaldehyde intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The research explicitly states that tetrose sugars are converted by beta-dehydration to an alpha-ketoaldehyde prior to sulfur/nitrogen adduct formation. This step is therefore included separately to avoid the over-compression criticized in prior attempts (Weber, 1987; Weber, 2001).

reactions.name: Surface thiol-assisted redox rearrangement of alpha-ketoaldehyde to activated threonine thioester

conditions.other: one-pot sulfur-rich pool, anoxic, mild pH

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-ketoaldehyde forms an imine-hemithioacetal adduct with NH3 and sulfur species, then undergoes intramolecular redox rearrangement to an activated threonine thioester.
reactions.reasoning: This step encodes the key mechanistic detail emphasized in the report: adduct formation with NH3 and H2S followed by redox rearrangement to an activated threonine thioester. It is separated from tetrose formation and from final hydrolysis specifically to address prior under-specification (Weber, 1987; Weber, 2001).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of activated threonine thioester to threonine

conditions.other: cool prebiotic pool or clay-water interface; hydrolysis or transfer chemistry possible

reactions.mechanism: Activated thioester is hydrolyzed to the free amino acid under aqueous prebiotic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that the activated threonine thioester can hydrolyze to free threonine or participate in peptidization. This is one of the most direct literature-supported terminal steps to the target and is placed in the Biochemical setting to reflect post-surface assembly and preservation in cooler concentrated pools (Weber, 1987; Weber, 2001).

reactions.name: Surface cyanamide generation compatible with cyanosulfidic network
reactions.mechanism: Concentration and dehydration of cyanide/ammonia derivatives generate cyanamide as a condensing co-reagent.
reactions.reasoning: The deep report highlights cyanamide as a mutually compatible condensing agent within the same broad prebiotic systems chemistry that generates amino acids and ribonucleotide precursors. This node is included to connect the sugar and amino acid subnetworks, although the exact geochemical route here is more connective and less directly specified than the core threonine steps (Sutherland, 2016; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical cyanamide-assisted hydrolysis/activation of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool with staged activation chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Cyanamide-mediated activation promotes nitrile hydration/hydrolysis sequence toward the free amino acid in condensed systems chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This is a connective extension of the report's compatibility argument: cyanamide is identified as a condensing agent coexisting with amino acid precursor chemistry, so its use here as an activating co-reagent for aminonitrile conversion is plausible but more speculative than the direct hydrolysis step. It is retained to create one of the requested alternative paths without contradicting the literature core (Sutherland, 2016; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Photochemical Mg-porphin-catalyzed threonine formation from cyanoacetylene, CO, water, and hydrogen

conditions.other: strong UV/high-energy radiation, stereocontrolling bicyclic photochemical intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited Mg-porphin forms charge-transfer complexes with cyanoacetylene and CO, enabling cyclization, water addition, and stereocontrolled assembly of threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The deep report specifically cites Aylward's computational Mg-porphin model for L-threonine formation from propyne nitrile, CO, water, and hydrogen via photochemical activation and bicyclic stereocontrol. This route is explicitly acknowledged as theoretical and more speculative than the surface cyanosulfidic and tetrose-thioester routes, but it is literature-grounded rather than invented (Aylward, 1999).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic aminonitrile route
pathways.description: HCN undergoes UV/H2S/Cu-driven reductive homologation to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde; triose chemistry yields threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route through dihydroxyacetone pool
pathways.description: The triose network branches through glyceraldehyde isomerization to dihydroxyacetone before delivering threonine aminonitrile and threonine.

pathways.name: Prebiotic sugar pathway via tetrose pool and thioester
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde form tetroses, which dehydrate to alpha-ketoaldehydes and rearrange with NH3/H2S to activated threonine thioester that hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal aldehyde feed into sugar-thioester route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon chemistry supplies acetaldehyde-related reduced carbon and supports interconnected surface aldehyde chemistry feeding the tetrose-thioester pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate feed into surface sugar-thioester route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction generates reduced C1 inventory represented by formate, supporting connected surface carbon chemistry that leads to tetroses and then threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with cyanamide-assisted completion
pathways.description: The canonical aminonitrile route is coupled to cyanamide-compatible activation chemistry in concentrated pools to furnish threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Vent chemistry supplies reduced carbon and nitrogen-compatible organics to surface pools where cyanosulfidic homologation and aminonitrile formation generate threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate/alanine supported sugar route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal protometabolic chemistry generates pyruvate and alanine; alanine-compatible mild catalysis supports tetrose formation and downstream thioester-mediated threonine production.

pathways.name: DHA-enriched cyanosulfidic branch with cyanamide-assisted release
pathways.description: A triose pool enriched by glyceraldehyde-dihydroxyacetone isomerization branches to threonine aminonitrile, then uses cyanamide-compatible activation for conversion to threonine.

pathways.name: Photochemical Mg-porphin direct route
pathways.description: A literature-mentioned theoretical pathway uses cyanoacetylene, CO, hydrogen, water, and photochemically activated Mg-porphin to form stereocontrolled threonine directly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature alignment with the Sutherland-style cyanosulfidic network: HCN/H2S/UV to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde is well supported, and threonine aminonitrile as a branch-product is consistent with the cited deep research. The main weakness is that direct hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine is plausible but underspecified and may compete with slower or side hydrolysis pathways.
suggestions: Specify hydrolysis conditions for threonine aminonitrile, including pH, temperature, and whether mineral-assisted hydrolysis is assumed.
Clarify whether the product is a threonine/allothreonine mixture rather than a single stereoisomer.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best pathways in the network. The glyceraldehyde to dihydroxyacetone isomerization is directly supported by the literature summary, and the triose pool feeding threonine aminonitrile is chemically coherent. As with P1, the terminal aminonitrile-to-threonine conversion needs better specification.
suggestions: Make explicit that the DHA/glyceraldehyde pool is the source of the observed threonine precursor branch chemistry.
Address likely diastereomeric output and possible protection/stabilization on clay or in cool pools.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good match to the prebiotic sugar/thioester literature. The tetrose pool to alpha-ketoaldehyde to threonine thioester sequence is consistent with the reported dehydration and redox-rearrangement concept. The main concerns are lack of explicit thiol reagent identity in the thioester-forming step and some ambiguity in using ammonia as a participant in aldol/tetrose-pool formation.
suggestions: Replace generic NH3 involvement in rxn_010 with catalytic wording if ammonia is intended as catalyst rather than stoichiometric reagent.
Specify the thiol catalyst or sulfur donor more clearly in rxn_012.
Indicate that the tetrose node is a pool containing erythrose/threose/erythrulose, not a single defined compound.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The downstream sugar-thioester logic is reasonable, but the hydrothermal feeder step to acetaldehyde is weakly justified and not well aligned with the literature emphasis for threonine. The stated pathway name suggests hydrothermal aldehyde feed, but the network as summarized does not robustly connect acetaldehyde into the tetrose route, making this pathway less coherent than claimed.
suggestions: Show an explicit reaction converting hydrothermal acetaldehyde into a surface-relevant precursor actually used in the tetrose pathway.
Avoid overreliance on speculative hydrothermal acetaldehyde generation if literature support is limited.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal formate production is plausible and well grounded, but its role as a feeder into the sugar-thioester route is not explicitly realized in the listed reactions. As a named pathway, this looks more conceptual than fully instantiated in the graph.
suggestions: Add an explicit formate-to-formaldehyde or related feeder step if hydrothermal formate is intended to supply surface sugar chemistry.
Tighten the pathway definition so every named route corresponds to an actual connected reaction chain.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanosulfidic core remains strong, and cyanamide compatibility is indeed literature-supported in broader systems chemistry. However, the specific generation of cyanamide directly from HCN + NH3 is chemically weak as written, and its precise mechanistic role in converting threonine aminonitrile to threonine is too vague.
suggestions: Use a more realistic cyanamide source or clarify that cyanamide is exogenous or generated through another established route.
Specify whether cyanamide acts as condensing agent, pH modifier, or activation partner in aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the intended hydrothermal-to-surface flow, but the hydrothermal contribution is not chemically integrated into the cyanosulfidic sequence unless HCN sourcing is assumed externally. As a network motif it is sensible, but as a specific route it is underconnected.
suggestions: Insert an explicit link between hydrothermal products and HCN/surface feedstocks to make the environmental transition mechanistically meaningful.
Distinguish clearly between environmental co-availability and actual precursor conversion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyruvate and alanine are metabolically relevant hydrothermal products, but their stated support for the sugar route is not concretely represented in the reactions. The hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis itself is plausible only at a general level and lacks strong literature specificity relative to the target-focused threonine literature.
suggestions: Either add explicit reactions showing alanine/ammonia catalysis of tetrose formation or remove this as a distinct pathway.
Ground pyruvate synthesis with more specific mineral/condition support if retained.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a sensible variant of the DHA-centered cyanosulfidic pathway, but the same cyanamide-generation issue limits confidence. The literature supports DHA enrichment and compatibility of cyanamide in broader prebiotic systems, yet the exact route encoded here is not fully convincing.
suggestions: Provide a literature-grounded cyanamide formation step.
Clarify whether cyanamide is necessary or merely auxiliary to aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway reflects the literature-mentioned Mg-porphin computational photochemistry, but it remains highly speculative and appears mis-specified: the literature route involves cyanoacetylene/propyne nitrile, CO, water, and photochemical activation on Mg-porphin, whereas the network lists H2 and omits water from the node accounting. As encoded, it is not robust enough to support target formation confidently.
suggestions: Match the literature more closely by explicitly including water and photochemical excitation conditions.
Flag this route as speculative/theoretical and avoid treating it on par with experimentally grounded pathways.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a generally strong and well-connected synthesis network with good hub usage and clear convergence around HCN, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde/DHA, tetrose pools, and threonine aminonitrile. The best-supported parts align well with the deep research findings: cyanosulfidic reductive homologation, triose branching to threonine aminonitrile, and the complementary sugar-to-thioester route are all represented coherently. Environmental staging is also mostly appropriate, with target-forming steps shifted to cooler surface/biochemical settings in line with threonine instability at high hydrothermal temperatures. Major strengths are network topology, convergent design, and literature alignment for the core surface chemistry. Main weaknesses are that several named hydrothermal feeder pathways are only loosely instantiated, some hydrothermal reactions are generic rather than target-grounded, mineral-agent detail cannot be fully verified from the summary, and a few reactions are chemically underspecified or speculative, especially cyanamide generation and the Mg-porphin direct route. Overall, the network is viable and above threshold because at least the cyanosulfidic and sugar-thioester branches provide credible routes to threonine.
